segments.text: Great.
Do
you want anybody else to come and join you at the table, or are you-
I haven't been able to persuade any more than one so far.
But with your permission, Mr. Chair.
You're very shy in retiring all of you.
I meant to invite you in earlier, and...
As time goes on, depending on your questions-
They can come. Yeah
... we might call upon-
Sure
... particular subject matter experts.
Sure. But welcome. Welcome, all of you.
Good to see some familiar faces back again who've worked very hard on many bits of
legislation.
Yes, sir.
And we'll use your process. We'll open up for a few minutes of
anything you wanted to touch on, and then we'll go to questions.
Wonderful. Well, good afternoon, Mr. Chair and team.
Thank you very much for having me. I'm really looking forward to the opportunity to
discuss matters that are dear to all of our hearts, I'm sure, building and
construction.
I think I'll make my opening remarks pretty brief, because obviously we don't have
that much time together, and I'm just really keen to place myself in the hands of
the committee in terms of areas of interest for you.
I think I'd be remiss, though, if I didn't make three acknowledgments.
The first is that we've got a building sector in this country that has
many men and women who are hardworking, talented,
determined. They've faced a difficult time in the last few years, for many
of them, and different by different regions of the country and
according to their place in the process and so on.
But it has been a tough last several years.
But
my hope is that we can
reform our systems in a way that will make it easier and faster and more
affordable for them, of course, and for those who benefit from the built
environment in this country.
My second acknowledgment is of the committee itself.
You've worked very hard, if I may say, Mr.
Chair, you and your team, to provide great advice
and suggested changes,
all of which I think for many have been accepted in terms of legislation, and
goodness knows there's been a bit of that, and there may yet be more too,
through some combination of stages in the remaining parliamentary term.
So thank you for that. Genuinely grateful.
And I always make that point when I have the opportunity to give a second reading
speech in the house.
But allow me to-
We always appreciate it
... thank you. And the other one is the MB officials who have
been working on various items of reform,
I think I can say have been consistently hardworking, dedicated.
And the feedback I get from the sector about the level of
interaction and engagement from the MB officials has been
highly impressive. So I do want to take the opportunity to put it on record that
I'm grateful for them. And on that note, of course, you have a continuation,
effectively, of this session at the point that I depart, and you'll have a chance
to talk with them. So I don't want to steal their thunder in terms of anything
they'll say. And obviously, if there are organizational matters, I'll obviously
answer as best I can for the committee, but just to acknowledge the fact they'll be
available to you as well for the
technically second part of the hearing. But other than that, through you, Mr.
Chair, very happy to engage and of course answer any questions you may have.
Thank you. And thank you for your generous comments, and always thank you for your
generous comments during various debates when you...
So we always appreciate that.
Genuinely grateful, but thank you.
Thank you. Okay. I did offer the first question, and probably the
second question at least to Arena.
Thank you.
Arena, away you go.
Mr. Chair.
Yeah, you're welcome. One and two there first. No.
Thank you, Minister. You've got a busy program of reforms, and many of them we
agree with.
Yep.
And some we push you to go further and faster.
And we're coming from different perspectives.
I would have prioritized workforce constraints.
Your government constantly refers to planning system constraints as the
major ones. But I want to ask you genuinely About three things-
... that are something we find common ground on, but disagreement around the
edges in terms of whether they are improving constraints on
supply. The first is overseas product.
Yeah.
Last year you were saying that about 250,000 products will be approved by
the end of this year.
Yeah.
It currently looks like about 19,000.
This is a market that's long been characterized by duopoly, that's vertically
integrated,
has exclusive distribution arrangements in the New Zealand market.
Is this a regulatory problem at all? Is this a market structure problem?
Thank you for that.
Were you intending to simply ask me-
Yeah, I've got a couple ones
... the one question?
Yeah.
Okay.
The second one is granny flats.
Yeah.
Building small dwellings cheaply, we support that.
I think everyone around this committee wants that to work.
Thank you.
But the pipes aren't in the ground.
And MBIE projected out that between 9,000 and
12,000 were going to be built. Yesterday, the housing minister
couldn't tell me how many have been built. Yeah.
And the third question is about infrastructure building.
Probably the thing where we have the most agreement around the house, that this
needs to be bipartisan and long-term.
But New Zealand's market under your watch is concentrating.
A smaller number of firms are capable of undertaking large scale civil and
construction
work, particularly water infrastructure.
Specialist engineering is being lost in New Zealand.
Homegrown company building capacity is scarce now,
not even just looking at the future pipeline.
So it's raising prices at the top end of town, but will rise prices later.
That's going to create real issues for building and construction in coming years.
Great. Okay, thank you. Mr. Joell, answer those or
respond to those three questions in the same order that they were posed.
In terms of overseas products, I'm grateful for the support around the house for
the proposition that we need to have a more competitive supply of building
products.
Of course, overseas approvals doesn't mean that we want a greater proportion
even than the existing 90% of products that are from overseas
to be introduced. And certainly we don't want to make life more
difficult for local suppliers and manufacturers and no one would suggest that's
the case. But I feel the need to place that on the record,
for the sake of completeness. In terms of the number that have been approved, it's
always been the case that with a relatively new
regime that would need to proceed carefully, we wanted
to
take a conservative approach. So while of course the
overall impact of the regime is to open up competition by making it
easier for products to be approved in this country, nevertheless, we
don't want to throw the baby out the bathwater and of course risk any kind
of
lowering of quality, sustainability or safety of materials and
therefore obviously the built environment.
In terms of the numbers, it might be that our MBIE
friends are able to advise more particularly what the running total is.
But I know that there are a couple of big ticket items in terms of
certification systems overseas whereby credible and comparable
jurisdictions have whole swathes of products, similar
products in some cases, that are able to be approved.
So I'm not
bothered by the fact that we will proceed at the pace that we can safely
do so, because obviously we don't want to risk
any suggestion whatsoever that we would have lower confidence as a result of
materials being approved in this country that wouldn't have been approved if they
had jumped through all the hoops that we're trying to avoid.
And my final comment in that regard, if I may, is simply that
improving competition should place downward pressure on price, and over time that
will increasingly be so as the shipments come in.
Goodness knows, supply shocks and other external factors associated
with those will, of course, play a part.
But it's not just downward pressure on prices we're after, it's greater innovation,
including for the purpose of improved sustainability of products such as
we see in overseas jurisdictions that we can and should benefit from in this
country.
And of course, simply to allow more options and
choices for homeowners and those who would design and build their
homes and commercial environment and so on.
Can I just pause for breath in case there's a particular
statistical input from our friends at MBIE in terms of the number of products
approved so far?
Yep.
Did you want to-
So I did a quick calculation.
Did you want to introduce yourself?
Oh, apologies for butting in. Yeah. Sorry.
David Paul, I'm the General Manager of the Building System Performance branch at
MBIE.
Thank you, as the minister said, for the opportunity to talk and for all the work
you've done most recently on the earthquake building stuff.
It's a big program of work that we've
brought through to you.
So look, just to answer the question, and I guess first thing is a little bit of
a proviso. The way the scheme works,
as you're probably very familiar with, is that in some cases we approve standards,
and then there's a whole lot of products that are approved under that standard that
would then get the automatic entry into the New Zealand market.
For those standards, we have no idea how many products may be sitting
behind them and relying on that standard.
What we do know for the ones that are more product specific
is that there are slightly north of 130,000 products that
have been approved so far,
and there'll be a lot more under the various standards.
Now, those are products that have been approved.
They're not necessarily in the New Zealand market, but they are products that have
been approved through the scheme.
Thank you. That's really helpful in my view, to have that number,
which I wasn't fast enough to find myself.
Going to your second point,
Ms. Williams, if I may, in terms of granny flats, the estimate, and I do
emphasize an estimate, was that some 13,000 such
new-
accommodation
types whose statues were dwelling, although they are a dwelling, a minor dwelling
of sorts.
Extra 13,000 over 10 years. Obviously, we're very early in the
process from the point of view that it was only in mid-January of this year that
the rules came into effect, both in terms of the resource management side of
things, which is led by Chris Bishop rather than me, but
also on the building consent side of things.
So, in the intervening five or so months, I
know there have been a number of such granny flats constructed, and indeed
I was in northwest Auckland yesterday, my own electorate actually, I'm
happy to report, and attended the official opening of one such granny
flat. Now,
there's one here, there's one there.
Clearly the pipeline will build, but the word is getting out there.
And so obviously I can't
wield a crystal ball to tell the member exactly what the numbers will be
over 10 years, and it may be fewer, it may be more than 13,000.
But I think the principle of the thing is important, which is that we are
proving that it is possible to use existing infrastructure,
where there is already a residential zone and usage of a property.
And of course, it's a detached dwelling of a
single story up to 70 square meters on an existing parcel of land where there is
already an existing dwelling. So from that point of view, we think it's helpful
between that and the self-certified elements whereby these relatively low-risk
structures are signed off in effect by the licensed building
practitioners and other qualified tradies who are doing the work.
So we think it's helpful both in terms of system reform more generally, but
also the particular outcomes for those who might or might not be grannies,
per the retail description of granny flats, but they might also be younger New
Zealanders, they might be farm workers, there might be others,
students, au pairs, other boarders who can benefit.
And so I pass to the third and final question or comment
regarding infrastructure. Technically, to the extent that
civil construction and other engineering falls
outside the Building Act, that's technically not within my remit.
But I can say clearly there are market forces that
we need to be alive to. We don't want to have
a market in which there isn't
competition either for practical reasons or, heaven forbid,
ways that the regulatory regime would
force a concentration that you've described.
So we know that's something we need to be mindful of and certainly talking with
ministerial colleagues who are variously the Minister for Infrastructure
and the Minister for Commerce and Consumer Affairs, that's something we need to be
mindful of, for sure.
If I can ask a different primary question then.
Yep.
Just
the budget contains
no new spending, and reasonably significant cuts
alongside a pretty ambitious reform program.
So I'm trying to understand what are the
trade-offs, what are the things that lose out because of that?
It's a 6.1% decrease in
the vote whilst at a time where, say, safety
standards
are a real focus. Asbestos contaminated fire doors this year,
increasing lead taps, blind cords that pose a serious risk to children, failing
imported window systems that make walls leak.
Those things indicate that we have ongoing issues
in New Zealand's buildings around maintaining quality and safety.
I'm not expecting you to solve it all, but is it getting worse?
Good question. I think there's quite a lot in that
question.
To speak to blind cords, technically it's a matter of
safety as opposed to building construction.
So a ministerial colleague
will be able to answer questions you have in that space.
I won't
speak on behalf of Brooke van Velden who would be the relevant minister,
I think. But in any case, it's not me.
In terms of--
apologies, that might be standards, that might be Cameron Brewer.
But in any case, I can't help you specifically on that one, but of course, it's an
important issue. In terms of leaking of windows
or doors, these are not new issues or problems.
But the thing I'm really pleased about the approach that MBIE has taken is that
they are very mindful of the need to enforce strongly standards
that were already in place and remain in place, and our
broadening of overseas approvals pathways doesn't actually change
the fact that when we have a set of rules, they must be enforced, and they can't be
undermined by fraud and forgery. There have been recent
instances that have been determined, and I'm very pleased to say that MBIE
has initiated enforcement action against those
entities behaving in that way. Because of course that's not good for the consumer,
but it's also an exercise in creating a non-level playing field for
those New Zealand manufacturers in particular who work hard and play by the rules
and deserve not to be disadvantaged in relative terms.
So, I think clearly there will always be issues
against which we need to be vigilant in the
built environment performance systems.
But I don't think anything in terms of the current budget would undermine that.
Indeed, we are working hard to ensure that there is
enforcement, including of the
BIPA, the Building Products Information-
Requirements
... Requirements. Thank you.
It was 50/50 between that and regulation
or regime. Anyway, requirements it is.
And where there have been reductions in budget, that really reflects the
discontinuation of programs that are no longer needed.
For example, for the passage of time since
where the tight homes
processes were always due to play out and finish at that time.
That, I guess, is my question. Which parts of
the vote are the things that are not a priority for you,
where MB could be finding cuts? Because with time with
officials later, we'll be able to delve into what their plan is to make those
pretty ambitious cuts that are bigger than Treasury has ever let any
previous government book. But in terms of, is it the building consent system?
Probably not. Is it the occupational licensing and regulation system? Well, no.
We're seeing pretty hefty changes there. Is it product assurance?
You've said it's not. Is it building code?
There are no changes coming, but you've already got a big program of work on some
changes there, including to insulation and windows.
Is it standards work? Is it self-cert? Is it earthquake-prone building?
Yeah. I think I've identified one.
The other one is in terms of standards work, the ongoing work
needed to ensure that our building standards, as part of a general standards
regime, is fit for purpose and aligned with Australia particularly.
That will continue. What we did see in the most recent
couple of years is that a large effort has been made
to bring back together,
to re-joint New Zealand standards with Australian standards, because
over a period of time they drifted apart and that makes no sense
for
us relative to our trans-Tasman neighbor.
And with that work, having had a particular injection of cash over a two-year
period, and that having come to an end with us having played catch up,
effectively, the ongoing work in a business as usual kind of way will
continue now at a more modest level to ensure that
the building team plays its part to make sure that our standards are fit for
purpose, appropriate, modern, and aligned with Australia.
Just a-
Last one, yeah
... very brief question about that then.
I'm worried about the capacity of this team. This is a great team.
You constantly thank them for their work in your speeches.
We have been able to get some assurance to get on board with your program of reform
because it is work that is conducted to a very high standard.
You want to do insurance, you want to do joint and several liability.
Those are two things that flummoxed the Law Commission and MB in a previous
iteration. How are we going to do this stuff?
Are you going to your budget minister's meetings and advocating for this
department? Why are we seeing some of the biggest cuts to this
team being made in baseline savings, which will result in a 6% cut that's bigger
than Treasury ever lets a labor government make?
Yeah.
I
don't understand the final characterization about-
A 6% cut-
Yes
... sounds unachievable.
Well-
Is your chief executive out there saying that this is achievable?
Did you say that Treasury hasn't allowed previous governments to make cuts?
Because it's impossible to look at a department that's doing this much work and
say, "Where are you going to find the cuts?"
Well, I would say, as you've rightly acknowledged, they are doing the work.
They're doing it extremely well, and they've been recognized in official terms
by way of awards internal to MB as well as
the recognition I've quite rightly given them.
They're getting on and doing the work.
They are achieving things that previous iterations of MB could only have
dreamed about, including in terms of joint civil liability.
And you'll literally lose one in 20 of their staff under this current model.
As long as they continue to provide outstanding outcomes, then
I will be very happy. If that were not the case, then we would need to consider a
range of factors, of which resourcing would be one, but only one.
And in the meantime, I don't think you can argue with the product, and to me,
that's more important than a line item and a dollar sign, and an
FTE headcount. They're being smart about the way they're doing it, and I,
for one, am really grateful for that.
Okay. I've got a couple of supps. Dan.
Thanks, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Minister.
Just going back to the building products, that's fantastic news that
130,000 overseas products have been
approved. Any idea of just, I guess,
how do you get the information out there to the sector so that they actually start
taking up those opportunities? So what is the plan for going
to disseminate that as quickly as possible so that we can start, we as
consumers, and can start seeing the benefits of those
150,000 products?
Yeah. If I may say, I think that's a really astute question that Mr.
Bigweir poses because I've had to give a lot of thought to the fact that
we have on paper now a much more
enabling system. And yes, we've been careful
to ensure there are safeguards to make sure that we
don't lose the quality, safety, sustainability that we need.
But I think the main challenge is an understanding and
a willingness of those who
specify the products and building consent applications in the first instance.
Second, where there are changes that can be made
to provide a different or better or more
readily available product, that's traditionally been quite tough.
And some of that
is come... I'm sometimes told by those in the
industry that there are anti-competitive practices by certain providers.
I'm not a one-person commerce commission.
I can't determine that to be the case or not.
But I can say that
where every opportunity is available to speak to architects and design
LVPs, to talk to building consent authorities, and also
to amend the regulations that
we have now, whereby you can substitute in products more easily that
weren't specified. For example, plasterboard type A
may be less available due to supply shop
reasons, or turns out to be less affordable than
to substitute plasterboard type B or C, should be
much easier than it has in the past. We've made that change.
Whether or not that's broadly known and acted on is probably an
open question, which is why I say your query is a good one.
So that's ongoing work, I think, for all of us.
Great. Thank you.
Thank you.
Can I ask a supp on that?
You got a supp on that one as well. It's time. Do you have a supp?
It's around the standards.
Is that on the same issue?
Yeah, sort of.
Okay. Away you go. Okay, and then Dorina.
Minister, I just want to ask you about MBIE's stewardship of building standards.
I've had some really concerning conversations with stakeholders
recently.
They're concerned that MBIE's ability to update standards
in response to new information, knowledge, changes in design of
materials,
is just not responsive enough.
They gave me an example and maybe something you want to talk to officials about
later. I'm sure you can ask them after your session here.
They were told that to change a couple of words in a standard relating
to concrete could cost up to $50,000 in
MBIE time, that they would have to pay for that.
They've also described their concern that it took six years
following the Christchurch earthquakes to update some standards relating
to the way concrete performs. Now, I know that's potentially before
your time in this office here as minister.
But I'm wondering, do you have confidence in MBIE's stewardship of standards and
their ability to respond to the market?
Can I say,
I have confidence in the building systems and performance
team. They are the ones who I've interacted with on all these matters,
including that they have let me know the work that they've done with the broader
team, which sits outside building standards and performance in relation
to standards. So, I think in ministerial terms, and I'm not trying to reflect,
because obviously you raise an important issue.
I think that sits with Commerce and Consumer Affairs.
But the visibility that I've had from the building side has been really positive
in the time that I've been in the role.
I can't obviously speak to past performance.
I would say, of course, it's generally the case that
the sectors themselves tend to fund changes.
And there are advantages and disadvantages in that.
And of course, resources
could be made more available, and that would be a legitimate point to be raised.
And in fact, I think,
Honora Williams might have come close to making such a point, and that would be a
choice we could make, and that would
speed up things and place less of a burden on the sector to fund any such
changes.
Well, I think they appreciate they have a responsibility to develop the technical
knowledge and the technical support for a proposed change.
I think one of the concerns is that MBIE is not
necessarily responsive enough,
and that MBIE's own costs for carrying out their
evaluations appear to be excessive.
So have you heard similar reports?
That's not something of which I've had visibility.
I know generally speaking, there is always a tension between the idea that
the government, as opposed to the sector, would fund standards developments.
But more particularly than that, I can't be helpful.
It might be that that's something that MBIE would be able to comment on, whether
now, and I'll leave
of you and the Chair as to whether you invite that now or in the follow-up session.
I think if it's an issue that officials can deal with, let's do that with
officials.
Yeah.
Otherwise, it'll take too much of your time.
Yeah. Okay. Happy. Thank you, Mr. Chair.
Right. Okay.
Supplementaries on this. You've done right.
Okay, so, I just had one, which is just in terms of the granny
flats,
it might be one you can deal with very, very quickly.
I understand there were some issues with some sorts of paneling which
were intended to be caught but weren't. In other words, that they...
Yeah, and that's been fixed. Is that correct?
Yeah, I think-
I see some nodding going on in the background.
That's helpful. Thank you.
Yeah.
That's my answer then.
To which I would simply add,
we instituted the granny flats policy with the hope that it would
achieve certain aims, which we've discussed and with which you're well familiar.
It's been the case since then that we've been careful to ensure we listen
to the sector to make sure that if there are other
opportunities to broaden the scheme or the effect of the scheme without
undermining its purpose, then we would.
And in the case of structural insulated panels or SIPs, these
are a building
system that already have code mark approval in some cases, and
so it would seem anomalous not to be able to include those
in granny flat construction. But because of the way that we framed
the
reasonably basic, straightforward standard nature of
timber buildings, they weren't included in our original conception, which is why
we've-
It's a bit of an oversight, but we will get there.
I think it's a very fair summary, yeah.
Yeah. Okay, cool. Right.
Carlos.
Primary. And did I have another primary floating around somewhere off the state?
Okay. Okay. Carlos, where are you?
Thank you, Minister, for coming. We heard a lot of feedback from the sector.
One of the major issues is the workforce issues.
Yeah.
So how can budget 2026 actually support the role of the
workforce in this sector?
Yeah, thanks for that. I'm grateful for the question because of course, of all the
different elements that are needed to produce a great built environment for
Kiwis to live, work and play, and learn and do other things.
We need not only products, as we've been discussing, and processes,
going to the consenting regime. But of course, most importantly, people.
I think workforce are supported, first of all, when we have a
good set of rules that enable them to get on and do their work in as quick,
easy, and affordable fashion as possible.
We want to reduce the stress often on small business owners who are
tradies.
Not always, but often.
And so in that sense, I think the moves that we're making and the resource that we
continue to apply to those who are helping to formulate the rules is positive.
I know in the budget elsewhere, and that is to say beyond the
building and construction elements, there are
helpful moves to provide infrastructure.
Horizontal infrastructure in a way that is not relevant to the work that comes
under the Building Act, but of course, enables the provision of housing
developments and so on, which in turn is useful to the work that people need to do.
And of course, the work that people need to do is important because,
it's roughly speaking, one in 10 Kiwis are employed directly or indirectly in
property.
And of course, we understand the value of the sector To economic growth,
including in the regions, as well as all of those who need to have
residential, commercial, and infrastructure provided.
So for those reasons, I think there are good stories to be told in the workforce
picture, even as I acknowledge, of course, as I did previously, that there have
been difficult times for those in the sector, and there are lots of macroeconomic
factors that we can analyze and discuss, and even argue over, as far as that goes.
But I think
overall, I would say the outlook is positive.
The consenting numbers to the year ended April were 16% higher than the
previous 12 month period. So, while we don't want to
see a boom-bust cycle head towards a boom, we do want to see a steady upward
incline of growth, and so far, cautiously optimistic that's what we're seeing this
year.
Yes. I also want to follow up with one of the pilot program called Earn As
You Learn. So is Budget
2026 actually allocated more money to this pilot program
to expand to more region?
Yeah. That's right. I think, and it's helpful you've asked that question because I
think there is
a number of different things we can do to make sure we've got an educated
workforce. We do need an educated workforce in the trades because
increasingly,
we have technology available that is sophisticated.
Has the great
advantage of enabling huge productivity advances if we are able to
use it well, and from a regulatory point of view, we enable that in a sensible
fashion. But we're going to need educated Kiwis in the workforce
to do that. Part of the education, of course, starts at the compulsory
education sector. But beyond that, and for those who do
choose to move on quickly from school environment,
they might want to enter into a program such as Earn As You Learn.
Which is a little bit like an apprenticeship as a previous generation would have
known, but sometimes across different business providers.
So, I think that's a helpful move. And also, of course, going back
to the compulsory education sector, to have building and construction
qualifications towards what one can walk away from
school with, as a young man or woman wanting to make
a career in the building and construction trades.
That's a really helpful innovation, to my mind.
It's the work of Erica Stanford, not Chris Penk.
And while I'm at it, the work of Penny Simmonds in terms of setting up industry
skills boards with relevant industry-connected
expertise. Again, it's not my work, but I think it's part of that overall picture
that's going to be helpful for our workforce issues into the future.
Thank you.
Cool. Thanks.
Dave.
Thanks, Mr. Chair.
Just heading to the consenting systems
because that seems to has been a big bugbear for a lot of people out
there.
You've taken some stuff away from the building consent system,
so you don't need to go through it. But just want to get a sense on
your reform program to speed up building consents,
and then secondly, how you're monitoring
those changes. So are we actually seeing
a reduction in building consent authority processing times?
Any observations that you might have would be
helpful. Thanks.
Perhaps if you could sort of lay out the things that you'll be thinking of if you
get a free run at it over the next 12 months, what are
the kind of things that you'd like to see changed in terms of legislative program?
Yeah. Thanks very much, Mr. Chair, and to the member for his
question. I think there are lots of things we need to do in terms of building
consents processing. We want it to be faster.
We want it to be no less rigorous. We want the quality, but then the
speed and the affordability also improve, and I know that sounds like an
exercise in
trying to
have it all ways. But I think the lack of productivity we've seen in the sector, by
some measures being as low as 1984,
pre-internet, let alone AI, and with New Zealand having a
much less
voluminous built environment, seems to me we can do much better.
I think the answer is to ensure that we have a balanced, proportionate
risk-based system.
And so the reforms that I'm about to describe, as you've invited, are
all geared around thinking about where there is work that is
bespoke,
or otherwise risky, or those doing the work are less
qualified or experienced or able to stand behind their own
work.
